content:
# Numbers Chapter 30 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 185-186; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. pa_0137 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_030_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_030_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_030_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

## The Law of Vows: Introduction (vv. 1-2)

**1** And Moses spoke to the heads of the tribes of the sons of Israel, saying, "This is the word that YHWH has commanded:

**2** When a man vows a vow to YHWH, or swears an oath to bind a binding upon his soul, he shall not profane his word; according to all that goes out from his mouth he shall do.

---

## Vows of an Unmarried Daughter (vv. 3-5)

**3** And when a woman vows a vow to YHWH and binds a binding in the house of her father in her youth,

**4** and her father hears her vow and her binding which she has bound upon her soul, and her father is silent to her -- then all her vows shall stand, and every binding which she has bound upon her soul shall stand.

**5** But if her father restrains her on the day of his hearing, none of her vows or her bindings which she has bound upon her soul shall stand; and YHWH will forgive her, because her father restrained her.

---

## Vows of a Woman Who Marries (vv. 6-8)

**6** And if she is indeed given to a husband, and her vows are upon her, or the utterance of her lips by which she has bound her soul,

**7** and her husband hears, and on the day of his hearing he is silent to her -- then her vows shall stand, and her bindings which she has bound upon her soul shall stand.

**8** But if on the day of her husband's hearing he restrains her, then he annuls her vow which is upon her and the utterance of her lips by which she has bound her soul; and YHWH will forgive her.

---

## Vows of a Widow or Divorced Woman (v. 9)

**9** And the vow of a widow or a divorced woman -- all that she has bound upon her soul shall stand against her.

---

## Vows of a Married Woman (vv. 10-15)

**10** And if in the house of her husband she vowed, or she bound a binding upon her soul with an oath,

**11** and her husband heard and was silent to her, and did not restrain her -- then all her vows shall stand, and every binding which she bound upon her soul shall stand.

**12** But if her husband surely annulling annuls them on the day of his hearing, then all that goes out from her lips concerning her vows and concerning the binding of her soul shall not stand; her husband has annulled them, and YHWH will forgive her.

**13** Every vow and every binding oath to afflict the soul -- her husband may cause it to stand, and her husband may annul it.

**14** And if her husband is indeed silent to her from day to day, then he causes to stand all her vows or all her bindings which are upon her; he has caused them to stand, because he was silent to her on the day of his hearing.

**15** But if he surely annulling annuls them after his hearing, then he shall bear her iniquity."

---

## Closing Formula (vv. 16-17)

**16** These are the statutes which YHWH commanded Moses, between a man and his wife, between a father and his daughter, in her youth in the house of her father.

**17** And YHWH spoke to Moses in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho, saying,

---

## SOPHIA's Consensus Notes

**Versification:** The Masoretic Text (Leningrad Codex) numbers this chapter as 30:1-17. Verse 17 is a transitional verse leading into the Midianite war narrative of chapter 31. Many English translations shift the numbering so that MT 30:1 becomes English 30:2 (with MT 29:40 becoming English 30:1). The SOPHIA Bible follows the MT versification. EMET provided 16 substantive verses and marked v. 17 as a chapter-end marker; LOGOS provided the full 17 verses including the transitional verse; PNEUMA followed an English 16-verse scheme. The manuscript image confirms the MT reading, and the consensus follows the Leningrad Codex's 17-verse division.

**"Binding" (issar):** EMET renders issar as "binding," LOGOS as "binding obligation," and PNEUMA as "solemn obligation" or "binding obligation." The consensus adopts "binding" as the most concise rendering that preserves the Hebrew root-meaning of asar ("to bind, tie"). This maintains the cognate force when the text reads "binds a binding" (asar issar), paralleling the "vows a vow" construction.

**"Profane" (yachel):** EMET and LOGOS both render lo yachel devaro as "shall not profane his word." PNEUMA renders "must not break his word." The consensus retains "profane" -- the Hebrew chalal (Hiphil) carries the sense of desecration, not merely violation, and this gravity should be preserved.

**"Restrains" (heni):** All three translators distinguish between heni ("restrain/disallow/overrule") and hefer ("annul/nullify"). EMET and LOGOS use "restrains"; PNEUMA uses "overrules." The consensus adopts "restrains" as closest to the Hebrew root nua ("to hinder, hold back").

**"Utterance of her lips" (mivta s'fateha):** EMET renders "the utterance of her lips"; LOGOS renders "rash utterance of her lips"; PNEUMA renders "rash promise from her lips." The consensus uses "the utterance of her lips" -- the Hebrew mivta itself carries a connotation of rash speech (from the root bata), but adding "rash" as an adjective is interpretive gloss. The base noun "utterance" is sufficient.

**Infinitive absolute constructions:** In v. 6, hayo tihyeh is preserved with "is indeed given"; in v. 12 and v. 15, hafer yafer is rendered "surely annulling annuls" following the binding convention to preserve infinitive absolute with doubling; in v. 14, hacharesh yacharish is rendered "is indeed silent."

**"Forgive" (yislach):** EMET and LOGOS render "will forgive"; PNEUMA renders "will release her from guilt." The consensus uses "will forgive" as the standard rendering of salach, which is used exclusively of divine forgiveness in the Hebrew Bible.

**LXX Vaticanus confirmation:** The Vaticanus manuscript image (p. 137) confirms the chapter structure and supports the MT reading throughout. The Greek preserves the emphatic doubled-verb constructions and maintains the same legal distinctions between restraining and annulling. No significant textual variants affect the translation.

-- SOPHIA
